experiments.title: How a longer term campaign flight date impacted revenue and donor conversion rate in a Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Would launching with a longer term (and more budget over that term) impact donor conversion or revenue when compared to the traditional 30-day campaign launch timeline (and budget) setting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, Americans For Prosperity launched a new open letter acquisition offer to allow concerned Americans the opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress to tell them NOT to use taxpayer money to bail out the states.
In launching the new offer, they wondered if launching the new campaign to promote this offer would be best if it used a 30-day campaign timeline (and 30-day budget) vs. a 6-month timeline (and 6-months worth of budget) in the campaign settings.
No other variable was tested within this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we needed to turn this experiment off before it validated, the results were directionally accurate when we observed a +36% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 66%) and subsequently a 39.2% increase in revenue (LoC: 90%) when giving the campaign a longer period of time and budget setting.

Our net cost per donor also dropped by 35% for the 6-month campaign and ROI was up +40% when compared to the 1-month campaign type.

Key Takeaway:

Our hypothesis is that when showing the ad service platform (in this case Facebook) that we want to run a campaign, telling them that we're willing to advertise for 6-months (and subsequently, can spend 6-months worth of budget in total) on this new campaign tells the algorithm that they stand to make more money when showing your ad, compared to other advertisers that might also be bidding to serve ads to that same user.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you're a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don't have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn't performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the "lifetime budget" setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: How simplifying “Dear Reader” ads on news articles impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the “Dear Reader” ad experience for newsroom visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/24/2021 - 04/02/2021
As a part of on-site promotions of a recent open letter (petition), Americans for Prosperity wanted to experiment with simplifying their “Dear Reader” ads — which are ads that promote new offers and acquisition funnels at the end of news articles — by replicating the “simplified ad experience” that was so effective within their Facebook advertising campaign.
Would this simplified ad experience also improve results for their on-site visitors to their newsroom pages?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a nearly valid 89% level of confidence, we observed an increase in donor conversion rate of +390%. Because Americans for Prosperity was looking to maximize results on the limited time they had available to promote this offer, we decided to roll this out pretty quickly.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 832.5% increase with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Email visitors had a 390.8% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Returning  visitors had a 1.0% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
It would appear that using the simplified ad experience (presenting a question, with a "Yes" or "No" button alongside it as a call to action is also as effective in on-site promotions through the "Dear Reader" ads as it was on the Facebook advertising campaign.

experiments.title: How the “fear of failure” at a campaign’s deadline impacted donor response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the fear of failure will increase donor response rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2021
As a part of American’s for Prosperity’s year-end fundraising campaign, they had a final email appeal reminding the donor of an upcoming deadline (midnight) so as to inspire them to give.
We decided to experiment with a positive message (implying that as the deadline approached, that the reader’s gift right now could “put us over the top” of our goals) vs. a more negative tone that focused on the fear of loss (implying that “we, collectively, will not succeed” without the reader’s gift right now as the deadline is approaching).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 93.9% level of confidence (just shy of a significant result), we observed an increase of +374% in the donor conversion rate when using a negative tone (eg a message that emphasizes the fear of not hitting our collective goals without the donor's gift right now).

Subsequently, we also observed an increase in revenue from the treatment (negative tone) message of +898% (LoC: 85.8%).

This revenue increase was achieved by a combination of a +52% increase in the average gift size, as well as the increased response rate—which multiplied together to drive the revenue lift seen within the study.

Overall, the samples are pretty light and we'd like to further experiment with this approach again in future studies, but the results seem to indicate that the "fear of loss" within the treatment message drove a higher response and better generosity.

experiments.title: How a YOU-centered call to action impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: How will framing the ask in the context of what you—the letter signer—just did, believe, and are looking to accomplish by taking this action impact donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/09/2020 - 10/23/2020
As a part of the SCOTUS 2020 confirmation hearings for (now) Justice Amy Coney Barrett, Americans for Prosperity had designed a digital activism opportunity for citizens to sign a letter and send it to their senators to tell them that they approved this nomination and were urging their elected officials to represent them by voting to confirm the nominee when it came time to officially vote.
The letter-signing page, once completed, would redirect signers to a donation page where they would be asked to make a financial gift to support the continued efforts to confirm the nominee.
In reading the “control” message, we felt that it was very organizationally-centered, and thought: What if we provided a more you-centered call to action, but because there was so much traffic available, we also decided to test a treatment that outlined the services that are available to donors to the organization, as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 92% level of confidence, we were able to achieve a +50.9% increase in donor conversion rate for the 2018 variant (which was YOU-centered in it’s appeal to the letter-signer/prospective donor).

Although this didn’t fully reach the target 95% level of confidence, we decided to turn off the study and roll this treatment out to 100% of the traffic, considering there was only a week or so left in the confirmation hearing process, and therefore the promotional campaign.

The key learning here is that we should always focus on what action someone just took, and think about the reasons WHY they just took that action. If we channel this line of thinking, we can present to them a reasonable and compelling reason as to why they would make a donation after having completed that action.

experiments.title: How simplifying the reading level of an open letter impacted downstream revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Would simplifying the reading level of the open letter increase email signup, donor conversion or revenue?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/19/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, American’s For Prosperity made available to concerned Americans an opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress as they planned a government response to provide citizens aid, as well as focused stimulus dollars to “restart the economy” again after the pandemic.
The open letter had become a wonderful engagement piece for the housefile, so they decided to open it up to prospective supporters to give them the opportunity to add their name alongside tens of thousands of other signers, as well.
Because the open letter was so popular, they decided to put a donation appeal behind it and test acquiring new donors, and did so very effectively.
But as time went on, the open letter signup (or email capture) rates were beginning to decline, so they wanted to experiment with increasing email signups.
The original letter was carefully crafted by government affair and policy experts and when graded in a readability tool, it was projected to be at a 15th grade reading level. If the 5th grade reading level they tried previously was determined to be “oversimplified,” they then thought to try a 12th grade reading level in an attempt to bring it closer to the original letter contents.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We observed that with a 92.7% level of confidence, that this “simplified readability” open letter decreased revenue by -32.5%.
This decrease in revenue was driven by two factors:
A directionally valid 24% decrease in donor conversion rate (LoC: 80%), and
A 13% lower average gift for the fewer number of donors that gave through the treatment experience.
It’s worth noting that we did observe a fully validated increase in email signup rate on the simplified open letter by +12.7% (LoC: 100%). This is particularly interesting, because although it did facilitate more email signups, that greater volume of people moving through to the donation page were NOT as motivated as those coming through the higher grade reading level in the control experience.
This experiment’s findings seem to validate the original hypothesis, which was that the increased reading speed works against the psychological approach and learnings gathered by adding the mini checkboxes—in that we found when we can slow someone down as they move through the funnel, that they become more likely to read and consume the content within the open letter. And when they do that, they are more likely to convert as a donor on the subsequent instant donation page because they fully understand and align with the contents designed to grow their motivation.

experiments.title: How a longer term campaign flight date impacted revenue and donor conversion rate in a Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: Would launching with a longer term (and more budget over that term) impact donor conversion or revenue when compared to the traditional 30-day campaign launch timeline (and budget) setting?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/11/2020
As a part of the COVID-19 (Coronavirus) response efforts, Americans For Prosperity launched a new open letter acquisition offer to allow concerned Americans the opportunity to add their name to an open letter to Congress to tell them NOT to use taxpayer money to bail out the states.
In launching the new offer, they wondered if launching the new campaign to promote this offer would be best if it used a 30-day campaign timeline (and 30-day budget) vs. a 6-month timeline (and 6-months worth of budget) in the campaign settings.
No other variable was tested within this campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we needed to turn this experiment off before it validated, the results were directionally accurate when we observed a +36% increase in donor conversion rate (LoC: 66%) and subsequently a 39.2% increase in revenue (LoC: 90%) when giving the campaign a longer period of time and budget setting.

Our net cost per donor also dropped by 35% for the 6-month campaign and ROI was up +40% when compared to the 1-month campaign type.

Key Takeaway:

Our hypothesis is that when showing the ad service platform (in this case Facebook) that we want to run a campaign, telling them that we’re willing to advertise for 6-months (and subsequently, can spend 6-months worth of budget in total) on this new campaign tells the algorithm that they stand to make more money when showing your ad, compared to other advertisers that might also be bidding to serve ads to that same user.

If you think of digital ad campaigns as an auction (which is what they are) — and you, as a buyer, show up with a big briefcase full of money — the auction house knows you’re a qualified buyer, and will provide you with preferential treatment. Just like with an auction, and as a buyer with a big briefcase full of money, you don’t have to actually spend the money you came into the auction house with. So, if the campaign isn’t performing well, you can always turn off your campaign(s) or ads.

Further experimentation may be required to validate this hypothesis, however, it might be advisable to simply run your campaigns for a longer period of time, with the total budget (under the “lifetime budget” setting) to ensure that you can garner better efficiency, better prioritization, and better return on investment for the same dollar spent by simply appearing bigger than you really are.

experiments.title: How a pre-selected value on the gift array impacted donor conversion rate and revenue from acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding a “most popular” pre-selected value on the gift array impact donor conversion rate or revenue for acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/22/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking into ways to increase their donor conversion rate and revenue from the investment they were making in acquiring donors through instant donation forms online. In reviewing the forms, we decided to try adding a “Most Popular” pre-selected option in the gift array to see how it would impact these key metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This treatment improved revenue by 94.4% (with a 92.3% level of confidence). Additionally, the donor conversion rate was showing an increase of 29.5% (although the level of confidence achieved was only 66%). Although these results didn’t officially validate, the lift in revenue is certainly directional and the decision was made to roll this out to gain the apparent benefits of the treatment option.

This increase in revenue was largely driven by an increase in the average gift amount of +55%.

Additionally, the number of $50 or greater sized gifts acquired through the treatment option was doubled in the treatment.

When donors are considering giving a gift during the instant donation page in your acquisition efforts, one of the decisions they have to make is “how much should I give?” This “Most Popular” treatment appears to decrease the friction in the process of making a donation because the donors now have a “starting point” provided to them as to make a decision about “how much” to give.

experiments.title: How placement of an email capture before a quiz improve email registration rates for the Economic Freedom Quiz

rows.name: read the petition now - email capture last
read the petition now - email capture first

experiments.researchQuestion: Does an email registration form at the beginning of the Economic Opportunity Quiz improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/05/2019 - 04/06/2019
When we launched the Economic Freedom Quiz, we wanted to improve our email registration rate. When reviewing the user experience, the email capture was originally placed at the end of the quiz, so we wanted to move it to before the quiz started to see if we could improve email registration rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 92.1% level of confidence, we improved the email registration rate by 71.7%.
Our original thought was that the anticipation of finding out your score would lead to a higher rate, which is why it was originally launched at the end of the quiz, however it appears that the quiz takers after clicking would be more willing to give their email address to start the quiz.

experiments.title: How the Freedom Quiz button text on the button promotion impacted petition click rates

rows.name: yes, i’ll sign the petition now
read the petition now - email capture last

experiments.researchQuestion: Which button text at the end of the quiz will generate the highest click rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/03/2019 - 04/05/2019
Americans for Prosperity launched a new quiz, which led to a petition signature page. At the end of the quiz, we wanted to test which button text would generate the most signatures.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 88.5% level of confidence, the treatment text on the button increased petition button click rate by 77.9% over the control.
With our goal being to get as many people from the quiz to the petition (and subsequently the donation page), our learning here is that we should err on the side of “soft asks” on button text. The control message forces the quiz-taker to decide whether or not they want to sign the petition before they’ve ever seen it.
Because the email capture rate is so low across both segments, we wanted to end this experiment quickly to launch an email capture at the front of the quiz experience (instead of at the end of the quiz) to increase overall email capture rates.

experiments.title: How reducing navigation affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing donation page navigation increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/02/2016 - 09/08/2016
Americans for Prosperity Foundation was preparing to launch a website redesign. In the few weeks they had before launch, wanted to run a quick donation page experiment to see if they could lift conversion.
The current donation page had a large navigation menu at the top that gave the visitor plenty of opportunities to leave the page before making a gift. They knew that removing these would prevent “unsupervised thinking” and they hypothesized that it would increase conversion.
They used Adobe Target to create a treatment with this navigation removed, and launched an A/B test to measure the winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with no navigation produced a 195.1% increase in conversion, although the results came just short of statistical validity. They ended the test before arriving at validity to launch the new site, although the test will be repeated with the new design.

experiments.title: Does adding a donor’s last gift into a supporter card renewal appeal increase renewals?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reminding a donor of their last gift date for members of AFP will achieve higher click throughs and donor conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/19/2022 - 03/05/2022
Americans for Prosperity has a long-standing membership card, which is promoted through mail and digital channels. For this year’s renewal campaign, we wanted to test whether including the image of the card, with a donors’ information overlaid on top of that image, coupled with a shorter copy, would lead to a higher click rate and higher donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Our treatment version with image and donor information performed better than the control version, with a 10% increase in click throughs. While not a valid result, it also resulted in 800% more donations than the control version.

experiments.title: How mystery impacted open rates of a webinar invitation email to high profile donors
experiments.researchQuestion: Would adding an element of mystery to the subject line increase open rates from these high profile donors?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/13/2020
Americans for Prosperity was looking to experiment with what ways they could increase high profile donor email engagement when promoting an upcoming webinar for them to attend. Because there was no way to register in the body of the email (but rather just “save the day/time”), the primary metric to measure was the open rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Regardless of which treatment these high profile donors were sent, the results were essentially identical, achieving only a 0.64% difference, with a 3.65% level of confidence (LOC).
At least when it comes to high profile VIP donors, that it doesn’t matter if you use mystery in your subject line to increase open rates.

experiments.title: How using a smaller gift array impacted donor conversion on acquisition forms
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a smaller gift array increase donor conversion rate on acquisition forms?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/31/2020 - 02/28/2020
In an attempt to increase donor conversion rate, Americans for Prosperity experimented with whether or not they were asking too much of 1st time donors. So, they wanted to test a smaller gift array to see if it would boost donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment didn’t validate (achieving only a 56% level of confidence), we noticed an 18% decrease in donor conversion with this experience.
We also noticed that revenue decreased proportionately to the decrease in the donor conversion rate, showing a 19% decrease, which was largely driven by less volume in the treatment experience, because the average gift was only about 2% lower for the treatment.
From here, we recommend further experimentation with variable gift arrays, but note that it may not have that much of a difference in terms of acquiring new donors.

experiments.title: How removing visual breaks in a page did not increase email signup rate on a pledge
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the design elements and visual breaks on a pledge page increase email signup rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/24/2019 - 11/08/2019
As a part of experimenting with what will increase email signups from the Freedom Pledge offer for Americans for Prosperity, we theorized that the visual breaks in the design may have been creating “mental hitches” for the reader that was limiting our ability to acquire emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for more than 2 weeks, there was not a significant enough difference between the control and treatment experience to validate this hypothesis, showing that there was a difference of less than 1%, with a level of confidence of only 6.75%, as well.
Further experimentation may be required.

experiments.title: Will asking for a gift to help us reach more people improve donor conversion rate?
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking prospective donors to help us reach more people increase the donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/11/2019 - 08/26/2019
In an attempt to improve donor conversion rates for the Freedom Pledge, we wanted to experiment with asking the prospective donor to help us reach more people to sign the pledge they just signed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment did not validate, we saw a slight decrease in donor conversion rate before we moved away from the offer altogether.
With only 4 total gifts between the two variations, more experimentation with the motivation of “help us get more people with your gift today” is advised.

experiments.title: How shortening a quiz’s first page content decreased email registration rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a shorter amount of text on the first (registration page) of a quiz improve email registration rates?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/29/2019 - 05/08/2019
When Americans for Prosperity launched their “Economic Freedom Quiz” offer, we wanted to experiment with what would boost email registration rates. In reviewing the first slide of the quiz, we noticed that there was a lot of copy, so we wanted to see if shortening it would keep more of the form on the visitor’s screen to improve the quiz start rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only a 69.4% level of confidence, we noticed a slight decline (-7.7%) in email registration rates.
Because this experiment did not validate and the difference between the two versions was so slight (< 10%), there is no key learning as a part of this experiment.
Further experimentation is advise.

experiments.title: How asking for support to extend our reach did not improve donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will asking Freedom Pledge signers to help us by funding our efforts to find more people like them outperform asking them to become a “donor activist”?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/26/2019 - 05/11/2019
As a part of Americans for Prosperity’s Freedom Pledge offer, we wanted to see if we could motivate donors to give towards helping us reach more people to band together with them to create a movement, as opposed to a straight-forward “become a donor activist” appeal to see if we could lift the donor conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Now that this version of the offer’s message is turned off, we turned off this experiment.
With only a 28.4% level of confidence, we did notice a developing trend of -14.3% on this approach. We did, however, also notice a +20% increase in revenue (with a level of confidence of 62%) with this approach.
Further experimentation may be required to determine the overall viability of asking this audience to fund reach, as opposed to asking them to become a “donor activist”.
It should be noted that this was related to the “original” Freedom Pledge positioning/language, which has been replaced, where overall, that messaging appeared to drive a higher level of instant donor conversion motivation when compared to the new messaging that we are required to use with the offer.

experiments.title: How using a primary issue on the main donation page increased donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Can moving a “defeat cronyism” -focused message into a single column layout outperform the two-column, generic message of “restore economic freedom” message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2019 - 04/21/2019
We wanted to experiment with improving the results of the primary donation form. In reviewing the “control” experience, we theorized that we could lift performance by addressing two issues:
Creating a more “issue-focused” message (defeating cronyism), and
Moving to a single column layout (instead of a two-column layout).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although the experiment ran for more than 3-months and we only achieved a 47.9% level of confidence, we did see a +15.0% increase in donor conversion rate.
We did notice that the treatment also saw a +60% increase in revenue (with a 71.1% level of confidence), as well.
Although neither treatment officially validated, we believe this information is directional enough (having run for 3-months) to move forward with the treatment donation page. It would appear that the primary issue the organization is largely focused upon solving (ending cronyism) is more inspirational, driving a higher conversion rate and average gift/revenue than the control message.

experiments.title: How a modified value proposition affected campaign performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which value proposition will have the biggest impact on revenue and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/28/2015 - 09/30/2015
In the final days of the September money bomb for Hillsdale College, we were looking for ways to improve results. After reading the original copy on the page, we decided there were areas that we could add additional clarity to. Our hypothesis was that by adding the additional clarity, we could improve the understanding of the value proposition which would lead to increased conversion rate on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings: The modifications made to the copy on the page had no statistically significant impact on either revenue or conversion rate. In this situation, it would appear that the changes were not radical enough to make a major impact. It is also worth noting that at the end of a money bomb campaign such as this, the motivation of the visitors is often high enough that subtle changes will not have a major impact.

experiments.title: How requiring all survey questions impacted the survey completion rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the survey response rate improve when all questions are not required?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2018
As a part of the 2018 calendar year-end fundraising efforts, we launched the National Concerned Citizen Survey, which was a 13-question survey designed to gather feedback from our donors, email subscribers and prospective donors regarding “how to align our priorities in 2019 and beyond.” We wanted to know if requiring all questions to be answered would negatively impact the survey completion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment did not validate and we decided to experiment with something that would likely more appropriately positively impact our ability to impact the survey completion (subsequently, the email registration) rate.
Further experimentation could be used to validate definitively, but it appears that requiring all fields on a survey of this length has a negligible of any impact on survey completion rates.

experiments.title: How social proof impacted the pledge signature rate.
experiments.researchQuestion: Does showing how many other people have signed the pledge increase or decrease the likelihood that someone else will sign the pledge?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/21/2018 - 08/29/2018
We wondered if showing the total number of pledge signatures to-date would improve pledge signature (or rather, email registration) rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With only 3% of the required sample size, we did not validate this experiment.
We stopped the experiment to pivot funds and focus upon a new opportunity to acquire names (SCOTUS confirmation efforts), which stopped traffic being sent to this page. Further experimentation is required to definitively answer whether social proof can accelerate registration rates.
It should be noted that the number of registrations (if perceived to be too few) may actually negatively impact the registration rates. Without being deceitful, it may be interesting to also test “large vs. small” social proof statistics and what lifts registration rates the most.

experiments.title: Does sending a newsletter on Friday instead of Saturday morning improve open rates?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does sending a newsletter on Friday instead of Saturday morning improve open rates?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/12/2018
AFP wanted to know if sending their weekly newsletter on Friday morning improved open rates over their standard Saturday morning delivery timeframe.
experiments.keyLearnings: Inconclusive. We need to create a larger sample and run the split again to conclusively declare a winner.

experiments.title: How simplifying and streamlining the value proposition impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will simplifying and creating an ordered “next steps” list on the instant donation page increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/24/2018 - 08/23/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an email acquisition campaign around the upcoming Supreme Court nominations. After visitors signed an open letter to the Senate, they were presented with an instant donor conversion page. We had seen relatively low donor conversion so we wanted to test out a bit of a radical change to the donation page.
Based upon experiments with similar organizations, we had found success in creating an ordered “next steps” list that walked visitors through what to expect and why a donation to AFP was so important. We decided to implement this and then remove some of the details of the full “plan” to reduce the friction associated with reading the whole page.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for a month, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the two pages. After further analysis of the two value propositions, one aspect of the pages that remained the same was the focus on the “product-level” value proposition of the efforts behind the Kavanaugh confirmation hearings. It may be worth testing a value proposition that is not so narrowly focused and, instead, emphasizes the overall efforts of the organization. This will be the next step in our efforts to optimize this campaign.

experiments.title: How a product-level value proposition performs on a donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a product-level value proposition, focused on the Open Letter, increase the overall donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/15/2018
Americans for Prosperity were running an open letter to the US Senate in regards to the upcoming confirmation hearings for Judge Brett Kavanaugh. After every signature, visitors were taken to a donation page for the option of making an instant donation. Given the emphasis put on this acquisition effort, we wanted to identify the optimal value proposition that would lead to the best overall donor conversion.
With this in mind, we wanted to test two different value propositions. The control focused more about the overall goal of the campaign and all of the effort that was going to go into getting the Senate to confirm Judge Kavanaugh. The treatment version focused more on the Open Letter. The hypothesis was that since the visitor had just taken part in the Open Letter, they may be more likely to support a value proposition that centered around it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While this experiment did not validate, after running for over a week, we did not see a significant improvement in the donor conversion. Given the time-sensitive nature of this campaign, we decided to turn off this campaign and move on to another hypothesis.
While we did not determine, with statistical significance, whether the product-level value proposition would outperform something that was a bit broader, it was unlikely that the product-level value proposition would outperform the control. Based upon that, we decided to move to the next hypothesis.

experiments.title: How a banner image impacts email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will removing the banner increase the visual clarity of the headline leading to increased email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/06/2018 - 07/17/2018
For the Open Letter to the Senate for the confirmation of Judge Kavanaugh, we had a highly converting email acquisition page. However, we had the hypothesis that it was possible the headline (which is one of the most important aspects of the landing page) was getting lost in the banner image at the top. To validate this hypothesis, we set up two versions of the page with the treatment including the exact same headline but placed inline with the copy with the banner removed.
experiments.keyLearnings: We saw no statistically significant difference between the control and the treatment. In this situation, it would appear the headline was not as difficult to read as we initially thought it might be.

experiments.title: How 3rd-party credibility impacts conversion at the point of conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including the 3rd party evidential of “3.2 million other Americans” decrease anxiety and, subsequently, increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/02/2018 - 07/05/2018
Americans for Prosperity (AFP) was launching an open letter to the US Senate regarding the upcoming Supreme Court nominee as a means to acquire new emails and new donors. AFP has a unique aspect of their organization in that they have 3.2 million activists that are a part of their efforts. We had the hypothesis that including this fact near the point of conversion (the submit button) would help decrease the anxiety associated with giving their information. We decided to test this.
experiments.keyLearnings: After showing the two treatments to over 5,000 visitors, we did not see any significant increase in a visitors likelihood to convert. It is worth pointing out that with a 65% conversion rate on the page, the visitors are already highly motivated. It is our hypothesis that, given this high motivation, the visitors likely do not have a significant degree of anxiety which means the 3rd party evidential was not necessary.
